title: Infections in Transplant Recipients
section: Clinical Syndromes Health Care Associated Infections

items.subtopic: Infections in Transplant Recipients | GENERAL CONCEPTS
items.title: Increased Risk of Infection Due to Immunosuppression in SOT and VCA Recipients
items.content: Recipients of solid organ transplants (SOT) and vascular composite allografts (VCA) are at increased risk for infections due to lifelong immunosuppressive therapy required to prevent rejection. This immunocompromised state predisposes them to both common pathogens and opportunistic microorganisms, which can lead to unusual presentations and multiple concomitant infections.
keyPoints: SOT and VCA recipients require lifelong immunosuppression.
Increased risk for both common and opportunistic infections.
Potential for unusual infection presentations and multiple concurrent infections.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · Infections in Transplant Recipients | GENERAL CONCEPTS

items.subtopic: Infections in Transplant Recipients | GENERAL CONCEPTS
items.title: Differences in Infection Risks Between SOT/VCA and HCT
items.content: While both solid organ transplants (SOT) and vascular composite allografts (VCA) involve surgical procedures with associated early infection risks, hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HCT) is primarily linked to infections due to neutropenia from conditioning regimens. SOT and VCA require lifelong immunosuppression, whereas autologous HCT does not typically use immunosuppression post-transplant, and allogeneic HCT uses it only temporarily.
keyPoints: SOT and VCA involve surgical procedures with early infection risks related to surgery.
Early infections in HCT are due to neutropenia from conditioning regimens.
Lifelong immunosuppression is needed for SOT and VCA, unlike autologous HCT.
Allogeneic HCT involves temporary immunosuppression.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · Infections in Transplant Recipients | GENERAL CONCEPTS

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS AFTER HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION
items.title: Understanding the Risk and Management of Infections Post-HCT
items.content: Infections are a significant cause of morbidity and mortality in patients undergoing hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HCT). The risk varies between autologous (auto-HCT) and allogeneic (allo-HCT) transplants, with allo-HCT having a higher infection-related death rate due to additional factors like GVHD. Understanding the timing, risk factors, and preventive measures is crucial for managing these infections.
keyPoints: Infections account for 15-25% of deaths in the first 100 days post-auto-HCT and 20-40% post-allo-HCT.
Risk factors include preexisting conditions, conditioning regimen-induced neutropenia, mucositis, immunosuppression, and GVHD (specific to allo-HCT).
Preventive strategies involve managing immunosuppression and monitoring for early signs of infection.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · INFECTIONS AFTER HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS AFTER HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION
items.title: Graft-Versus-Host Disease (GVHD) in Allo-HCT: Incidence and Prevention
items.content: GVHD is a significant complication of allo-HCT, affecting 35-50% of recipients. It arises when the donor's immune cells recognize the recipient's tissues as foreign, leading to an immune response against them. Preventing GVHD involves immunosuppression post-transplant, which is gradually tapered over months.
keyPoints: GVHD affects 35-50% of allo-HCT recipients with varying severity.
Prevention includes several months of immunosuppression followed by gradual tapering.
GVHD does not occur in auto-HCT due to the use of the recipient's own stem cells.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · INFECTIONS AFTER HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS AFTER HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION
items.title: Indications and Outcomes for Auto-HCT vs. Allo-HCT
items.content: Auto-HCT is primarily used for malignancies like multiple myeloma and lymphoma, where the stem cells are not defective. It serves to replenish bone marrow after intensive conditioning. Allo-HCT can potentially cure genetic disorders by replacing defective stem cells with healthy ones from a donor, leveraging the graft-versus-leukemia effect.
keyPoints: Auto-HCT is mainly used for multiple myeloma and lymphoma, not involving stem cell defects.
Allo-HCT treats conditions like acute leukemia, aplastic anemia, hemoglobinopathies, and primary immunodeficiencies.
Graft-versus-leukemia effect in allo-HCT can help eradicate chemotherapy-refractory malignancies.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · INFECTIONS AFTER HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION

items.title: Understanding Very Early Infections Post-HCT
items.content: The very early phase of hematopoietic cell transplantation (HCT) spans from the initiation of conditioning to neutrophil engraftment. This period is characterized by bacterial infections due to neutropenia and mucosal barrier injury, with consideration for preexisting or newly acquired infections.
keyPoints: Very early phase: Initiation of conditioning to neutrophil engraftment
Bacterial infections predominate during this time
Consideration for preexisting (e.g., cryptosporidiosis in SCID) and new infections (e.g., pulmonary aspergillosis)
Pretransplant infectious diseases consult recommended
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 1)

items.title: Management of Neutropenic Fever in HCT
items.content: Neutropenic fever is common during the very early phase post-HCT and is presumed infectious. Management involves rapid initiation of broad-spectrum antibiotics, with adjustments based on microbiologic data or clinical changes.
keyPoints: Fever during neutropenia often indicates infection
Broad-spectrum antibiotics including Pseudomonas coverage are initiated
Antibiotic choice depends on prior history and local resistance patterns
Persistent fever >4 days suggests possible invasive fungal disease
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 1)

items.title: Prophylaxis Strategies for Early Infections Post-HCT
items.content: Antibacterial and antifungal prophylaxis are used to mitigate early infections post-HCT, with fluoroquinolones and fluconazole being common choices. However, concerns exist about long-term consequences such as microbiome disruption.
keyPoints: Fluoroquinolone (e.g., levofloxacin) for antibacterial prophylaxis
Concerns: microbiome disruption, resistant bacteria, C. difficile colitis
Antifungal prophylaxis with fluconazole reduces invasive candidiasis risk
Posaconazole used if aspergillosis risk is high
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 1)

items.title: Prophylaxis and Management of Pneumocystis jirovecii Pneumonia (PJP) in HCT Patients
items.content: The risk of early PJP varies based on transplant indication, prior treatments, and conditioning regimen. Prophylaxis with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is standard until stem cell infusion, after which alternatives like pentamidine or atovaquone are preferred due to myelotoxicity concerns.
keyPoints: Risk of PJP varies by transplant indication and prior treatments.
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) is most effective for prophylaxis until stem cell infusion.
Post-infusion, alternatives like inhaled or intravenous pentamidine are preferred due to myelotoxicity concerns.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 2)

items.title: Management of Herpes Simplex Virus Reactivation Post-HCT
items.content: HSV reactivation is common early post-HCT due to mucositis. Acyclovir prophylaxis is standard, but breakthrough infections may require high-dose acyclovir or alternative treatments like foscarnet or cidofovir if resistance is suspected.
keyPoints: Acyclovir prophylaxis prevents HSV and VZV reactivation.
Breakthrough HSV should be differentiated from chemotherapy-induced mucositis using viral PCR.
High-dose acyclovir (10 mg/kg per 8 h) or alternatives like foscarnet (60 mg/kg per 12 h) may be used for resistant cases.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 2)

items.title: Risk Factors and Management of Bacterial Infections Post-HCT
items.content: Post-neutrophil engraftment, bacterial infections are less common unless GVHD occurs. Catheter-related infections remain a risk, while GVHD can lead to mucosal barrier injury and C. difficile infection.
keyPoints: Bacterial infections are uncommon post-neutrophil engraftment without GVHD.
Catheter-related bloodstream infections remain a risk.
GVHD increases the risk of mucosal barrier injury-related infections, including C. difficile.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 2)

items.title: Fungal Infections and Posaconazole Prophylaxis in GVHD
items.content: In the post-engraftment phase after hematopoietic cell transplantation (HCT), fungal infections are often linked to corticosteroid use for treating graft-versus-host disease (GVHD). Aspergillosis is a common mold infection during this period. Patients with GVHD on high-dose corticosteroids or multiple immunosuppressive agents may receive posaconazole prophylaxis, as supported by randomized controlled trials.
keyPoints: Fungal infections in post-engraftment phase are linked to corticosteroid use for GVHD treatment.
Aspergillosis is the most common mold infection during this period.
Posaconazole prophylaxis is recommended for patients on >0.5 mg/kg of prednisone every other day or multiple immunosuppressive agents.
Monitoring serum posaconazole levels, with <700 ng/μL considered subtherapeutic, though optimal levels are debated.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 3)

items.title: HHV-6 Reactivation and Management in Post-HCT Patients
items.content: Human herpesvirus 6 (HHV-6) reactivation is common after HCT, often asymptomatic but can lead to severe encephalitis. This condition presents with limbic encephalitis symptoms such as short-term memory loss, tremor, and potentially progresses to seizures or coma. Diagnosis involves detecting HHV-6 in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) when other causes are ruled out.
keyPoints: HHV-6 reactivation is frequent post-HCT but usually asymptomatic.
Severe cases can cause limbic encephalitis with symptoms like memory loss and tremor, potentially progressing to seizures or coma.
Diagnosis involves detecting HHV-6 in CSF when no other explanation for encephalitis exists.
Treatment recommendations include ganciclovir or foscarnet, sometimes combined; however, outcomes are often poor despite treatment.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 3)

items.title: BK Virus Infection and Its Complications Post-HCT
items.content: BK virus is associated with hemorrhagic cystitis post-allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation (allo-HCT), presenting with symptoms like dysuria, bladder spasms, and hematuria. Diagnosis can be complicated by the use of cyclophosphamide for GVHD prophylaxis, which also causes similar symptoms.
keyPoints: BK virus infection post-HCT is linked to hemorrhagic cystitis in 80–90% of people.
Symptoms include dysuria, bladder spasms, and hematuria, with high-level BK viruria often present.
Diagnosis can be challenging due to cyclophosphamide use for GVHD prophylaxis causing similar symptoms.
No known effective treatment exists; hemorrhagic cystitis is usually self-limited but may require continuous bladder irrigations in severe cases.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 3)

items.title: Antiviral Treatment Considerations for COVID-19 in HCT Recipients
items.content: In hematopoietic cell transplant (HCT) recipients, antiviral treatment of COVID-19 is often considered due to their immunocompromised state. Nirmatrelvir-ritonavir can significantly reduce hospitalization or death risk if administered early in non-immunocompromised patients but may be impractical for HCT recipients due to drug interactions with medications like tacrolimus and sirolimus. Molnupiravir is an alternative option, especially when other treatments are not suitable.
keyPoints: Nirmatrelvir-ritonavir reduces hospitalization or death risk by 90% if given within 3 days of symptom onset in non-immunocompromised patients over 60 with comorbidities.
Drug-drug interactions make nirmatrelvir-ritonavir use impractical for many HCT recipients on tacrolimus or sirolimus.
Molnupiravir has an FDA emergency use authorization for high-risk adults when other treatments are not suitable.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 4)

items.title: Management of Respiratory Viral Infections in HCT Recipients
items.content: HCT recipients may require specific management for respiratory viral infections. Neuraminidase inhibitors like oseltamivir are commonly used for influenza, while ribavirin is considered for RSV if lymphopenia (ALC <1000/μL) is present. Adenovirus treatment options include cidofovir and brincidofovir, though established disease responds poorly to antivirals.
keyPoints: Neuraminidase inhibitors are the preferred treatment for influenza in HCT recipients.
Ribavirin may be used off-label for RSV if ALC is <1000/μL as a risk factor for pneumonia.
Cidofovir and brincidofovir have activity against adenovirus, but established disease has poor response to antivirals.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 4)

items.title: Toxoplasmosis Reactivation and Treatment in Allo-HCT Recipients
items.content: Allo-HCT recipients with positive Toxoplasma serology have a high risk of reactivation, occurring frequently within the first six months post-transplant. Clinical presentations can range from fever to severe disseminated disease. Diagnosis is via blood PCR or immunohistochemistry, and treatment typically involves pyrimethamine and sulfadiazine.
keyPoints: 8–21% of allo-HCT recipients with positive Toxoplasma serology experience reactivation within the first 6 months.
Toxoplasmosis presentations include fever, pneumonia, heart disease, brain abscesses, and disseminated disease.
Standard treatment is pyrimethamine and sulfadiazine; TMP-SMX may be equally effective.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 4)

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS FOR INFECTION AFTER SOLID ORGAN TRANSPLANT
items.title: How Immunosuppression Affects Infection Risk Post-Transplant
items.content: The level and type of immunosuppressive therapy significantly influence the risk of infection in solid organ transplant (SOT) recipients. Induction immunosuppression is administered immediately before transplantation, followed by maintenance therapy typically involving a calcineurin inhibitor or mTOR inhibitor, mycophenolate mofetil, and corticosteroids. The intensity of immunosuppression can be adjusted based on rejection episodes but generally continues for life.
keyPoints: Induction immunosuppression is given immediately prior to transplantation.
Maintenance therapy often includes a calcineurin inhibitor (cyclosporine A or tacrolimus) or mTOR inhibitor (sirolimus or everolimus), mycophenolate mofetil, and corticosteroids.
Immunosuppression intensity may be adjusted during rejection episodes but is usually lifelong.
The level of immunosuppression correlates with infection risk.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · RISK FACTORS FOR INFECTION AFTER SOLID ORGAN TRANSPLANT

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS FOR INFECTION AFTER SOLID ORGAN TRANSPLANT
items.title: Age and Infection Susceptibility in SOT Recipients
items.content: Age plays a crucial role in determining the susceptibility to infections post-transplant. Older patients are more likely to have encountered pathogens that can remain latent and reactivate, such as tuberculosis (TB). Conversely, younger patients face higher risks of acquiring primary infections from the transplanted organ, which tend to be more severe than reactivated latent infections like cytomegalovirus (CMV). Preexisting immunity in older individuals may offer some protection against certain diseases.
keyPoints: Older patients are more likely to have encountered pathogens that can remain latent and reactivate post-transplant (e.g., TB).
Younger patients have a higher risk of severe primary infections from the transplanted organ (e.g., CMV).
Preexisting immunity in older individuals may protect against certain diseases.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · RISK FACTORS FOR INFECTION AFTER SOLID ORGAN TRANSPLANT

items.subtopic: ☑ TIME COURSE OF INFECTIONS AFTER SOLID ORGAN TRANSPLANT (TABLE 148-2)
items.title: Early Post-Transplant Infections: Nosocomial and Donor-Derived Risks
items.content: In the first month post-transplant, infections are typically nosocomial and associated with surgical complications. Bacterial infections predominate, often involving multidrug-resistant organisms. Prophylactic antimicrobials are initiated promptly to mitigate these risks. Hepatitis C transmission is possible from positive donors during this period. Opportunistic infections may suggest donor-derived infections if they occur without other risk factors.
keyPoints: First month post-transplant infections are generally nosocomial and bacterial in nature.
MDR bacteria can be involved, necessitating tailored prophylactic strategies.
Hepatitis C transmission is possible from positive donors during this period.
Opportunistic infections without comorbid immunodeficiencies may indicate donor-derived infections.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · ☑ TIME COURSE OF INFECTIONS AFTER SOLID ORGAN TRANSPLANT (TABLE 148-2)

items.subtopic: ☑ TIME COURSE OF INFECTIONS AFTER SOLID ORGAN TRANSPLANT (TABLE 148-2)
items.title: Intermediate Post-Transplant Infections: Opportunistic and Reactivation Risks
items.content: From 1 to 6 months post-transplant, classic opportunistic infections like P. jirovecii and CMV are prevalent unless prophylaxis is used. Latent infections such as TB and endemic mycoses may reactivate, necessitating pre-transplant risk assessment and specific prophylactic measures. Viral infections (e.g., BK virus, adenovirus) and invasive fungal infections (e.g., Aspergillus) are common, with risks varying by organ type.
keyPoints: 1-6 months post-transplant period is marked by opportunistic infections like P. jirovecii and CMV.
Prophylaxis or preemptive therapy can significantly reduce these infection incidences.
Reactivation of latent infections (e.g., TB, endemic mycoses) requires pre-transplant risk assessment.
Invasive fungal infections are common, with risks varying by organ type.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · ☑ TIME COURSE OF INFECTIONS AFTER SOLID ORGAN TRANSPLANT (TABLE 148-2)

items.title: Infection Risks in Vascular Composite Allografts (VCAs)
items.content: VCAs, which include complex tissue units like muscle and nerve bundles, face increasing infection risks due to their intricate anatomy and natural microbial colonization. Bacterial infections are most common, necessitating prevention strategies similar to solid organ transplants (SOT), including updated immunizations, donor/recipient serologic screening, and targeted antimicrobial prophylaxis.
keyPoints: VCAs involve transplantation of multiple tissue types as a single functional unit.
Bacterial infections are most common due to complex anatomy and natural microbial colonization.
Infection prevention strategies include updated immunizations, donor/recipient serologic screening, and targeted antimicrobial prophylaxis.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · VASCULAR COMPOSITE ALLOGRAFTS

items.title: Opportunistic Infections in VCA Recipients
items.content: The risk of opportunistic infections in VCA recipients is heightened by the need for intense immunosuppression to manage frequent rejection episodes. Systemic viral infections, particularly CMV and EBV, are significant concerns, requiring prevention strategies similar to those used in other high-risk SOT recipients. Fungal infections can mimic rejection, highlighting the importance of accurate diagnosis.
keyPoints: Opportunistic infection risk is driven by intense immunosuppression due to frequent rejection episodes.
Systemic viral infections like CMV and EBV are significant concerns post-VCA.
Fungal infections may mimic rejection, necessitating histopathologic or microbiologic diagnosis.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · VASCULAR COMPOSITE ALLOGRAFTS

items.title: Understanding Expected vs. Unexpected Donor-Derived Infections
items.content: Expected DDIs involve known pathogens in the transplanted organ, while unexpected DDIs occur when undetected pathogens are transmitted. Preventive strategies and specific guidelines help minimize risks.
keyPoints: Viruses like CMV, EBV, hepatitis B, C, and BK polyomavirus can be expected pathogens.
Unexpected DDIs affect <1% of transplant recipients, mostly infections.
Transmission risk is higher in cadaveric donations due to incomplete donor assessment.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · DONOR-DERIVED INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Unexpected Donor-Derived Infections
items.content: Early diagnosis requires high suspicion, especially for opportunistic infections outside expected periods. Prompt action includes diagnosis, treatment, and communication with health authorities.
keyPoints: Unexpected DDIs often manifest within 90 days post-transplant.
Overall mortality from unexpected DDIs is around 15%, higher in fungal and parasitic cases (30–50%).
Clusters of infections in recipients from the same donor should alert clinicians.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · DONOR-DERIVED INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.title: Considerations for Donors with Active Infections
items.content: Active infections in donors can be transmitted to recipients. Living donors with active infections should delay transplantation until recovery, while targeted prophylaxis is effective for susceptible bacterial infections.
keyPoints: Living donors with active infections should ideally be treated before donation.
Targeted antimicrobial prophylaxis prevents disease transmission from donor bacteremia if bacteria are susceptible.
Bacteremia in deceased donors generally isn't a contraindication unless no effective treatment is available.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · DONOR-DERIVED INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.title: Importance of CMV Screening and Management in Transplant Recipients
items.content: Cytomegalovirus (CMV) is a critical pathogen for solid organ transplant (SOT) and hematopoietic cell transplantation (HCT) recipients. It establishes lifelong latency, primarily detected through PCR testing which quantifies CMV DNA in international units per microliter (IU/μL). While often asymptomatic, it can lead to CMV syndrome or tissue-invasive disease, with gastrointestinal involvement being most common. Active replication of CMV is linked to increased risks of bacterial and fungal infections, graft dysfunction, rejection, GVHD, and transplant-related mortality.
keyPoints: CMV is the most important virus in SOT and HCT recipients.
Seropositivity for CMV ranges from 30% to 97%, increasing with age.
Detection of CMV typically involves PCR testing, expressed in IU/μL.
CMV syndrome includes fever, malaise, leukopenia, atypical lymphocytes, and mild transaminitis.
Tissue-invasive disease requires histopathologic proof.
Gastrointestinal disease is the most common end-organ involvement after both SOT and allo-HCT.
Active CMV replication correlates with increased bacterial/fungal infections, graft dysfunction, rejection, GVHD, and nonrelapse transplant-related mortality.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · DONOR-DERIVED INFECTIONS (part 2) | CYTOMEGALOVIRUS (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Implications of CMV Viremia in Transplant Recipients
items.content: CMV viremia, often asymptomatic, can precede or coincide with tissue-invasive disease. Clinically significant infection is defined by invasive disease presence or a viremia level necessitating antiviral treatment. Historically, CMV end-organ diseases like pneumonitis and gastroenteritis were common in the first 1–6 months post-transplantation, causing high morbidity and mortality. Active replication of CMV also contributes to indirect effects such as increased rates of other infections, graft dysfunction, acute rejection, GVHD, and nonrelapse transplant-related mortality.
keyPoints: CMV viremia is usually asymptomatic but can lead to significant disease.
Clinically significant infection includes invasive disease or high viremia levels triggering treatment.
End-organ diseases like pneumonitis and gastroenteritis were common in early post-transplant months.
Active CMV replication increases risks of bacterial/fungal infections, graft dysfunction, acute rejection, GVHD, and nonrelapse mortality.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · DONOR-DERIVED INFECTIONS (part 2) | CYTOMEGALOVIRUS (part 1)

items.title: CMV Risk Based on Serostatus Combinations
items.content: The risk of CMV disease in transplant recipients is primarily determined by the serostatus of both donor and recipient. In solid organ transplantation (SOT), a seropositive donor to a seronegative recipient (D+/R-) poses the highest risk, with up to 50% becoming viremic post-prophylaxis. For hematopoietic cell transplant (HCT), the highest risk is in seropositive recipients receiving stem cells from seronegative donors (D-/R+), where 60-70% may develop CMV viremia.
keyPoints: Highest SOT risk: D+/R-, up to 50% become viremic post-prophylaxis
Highest HCT risk: D-/R+, 60-70% develop CMV viremia
Avoid high-risk serostatus combinations during donor selection
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · CYTOMEGALOVIRUS (part 2)

items.title: Strategies for Preventing CMV Disease Post-Transplant
items.content: Post-transplant CMV disease prevention can be achieved through universal prophylaxis or preemptive management. Universal prophylaxis involves administering antivirals like valganciclovir (SOT) or letermovir (allo-HCT) for a set period, while preemptive management monitors CMV levels and initiates treatment upon detecting specific viremia thresholds.
keyPoints: Universal prophylaxis: Valganciclovir (SOT), Letermovir (allo-HCT)
Preemptive management: Weekly blood monitoring, treat on detection of threshold
Valganciclovir covers HSV/VZV; letermovir requires additional acyclovir/valaciclovir
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · CYTOMEGALOVIRUS (part 2)

items.title: Management and Treatment of Significant CMV Viremia
items.content: Significant CMV viremia is treated with ganciclovir or foscarnet. Cidofovir and maribavir are less effective, with cidofovir being potentially more toxic. If viremia persists after 2 weeks of treatment, the infection may be refractory, necessitating genetic resistance testing. Maribavir is approved for resistant/refractory CMV but shows moderate efficacy.
keyPoints: Treat significant viremia with ganciclovir or foscarnet
Ganciclovir: Myelotoxicity; Foscarnet: Electrolyte abnormalities, nephrotoxicity
Refractory CMV: Test for genetic resistance after 2 weeks of treatment
Maribavir approved for resistant/refractory CMV, <20% control at 16 weeks
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · CYTOMEGALOVIRUS (part 2)

items.title: Risk and Management of PTLD in EBV D+/R- SOT Recipients
items.content: In solid organ transplant (SOT) recipients who are EBV-seronegative receiving an allograft from an EBV-seropositive donor, primary EBV infection is common. This scenario increases the risk of post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder (PTLD), especially within the first year post-SOT and in pediatric patients. Monitoring EBV viral load can aid early diagnosis, with preemptive reduction of immunosuppression recommended for viremic patients.
keyPoints: EBV infection common in D+/R- SOT recipients
PTLD risk highest within the first year post-SOT
Intestinal transplant recipients at highest PTLD risk
Reduction of immunosuppression is initial treatment strategy
Preemptive monitoring of EBV viral load suggested for high-risk patients
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · EPSTEIN-BARR VIRUS

items.title: EBV Monitoring and Imaging in PTLD Diagnosis
items.content: For early diagnosis of EBV-associated PTLD, serial monitoring of EBV viral load is crucial. Positron emission tomography/computed tomography (PET/CT) imaging helps establish lymph node or end-organ involvement, guiding targeted tissue sampling for diagnosis.
keyPoints: Serial monitoring with the same assay and laboratory necessary
Rapidly rising EBV viral load significant risk factor for PTLD
PET/CT useful in establishing disease involvement
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · EPSTEIN-BARR VIRUS

items.title: EBV-Positive PTLD Management in Allo-HCT
items.content: In allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation (allo-HCT), EBV-positive PTLD occurs early, with a frequency of ~3%, varying by donor type. T-cell depletion increases risk; management includes reducing immunosuppression and using donor lymphocyte infusion or adoptive immunotherapy with EBV-specific T cells.
keyPoints: EBV-positive PTLD is an early complication in allo-HCT
Frequency of ~3%, higher in unrelated cord blood transplants
Donor lymphocyte infusion can be effective if the donor is EBV positive
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · EPSTEIN-BARR VIRUS

items.title: BK Virus Nephropathy: Diagnosis and Management in Kidney Transplant Recipients
items.content: BK virus (BKV) is a frequent cause of allograft dysfunction in kidney transplant recipients, often leading to polyomavirus-associated nephropathy (PVAN). Viruria is common among these patients; however, some progress to viremia and renal dysfunction. The presumptive diagnosis involves identifying renal dysfunction alongside viremia, while definitive diagnosis requires histopathologic confirmation via viral cytopathic changes or immunohistochemistry for SV40 or BKV-specific antigens in tissue samples.
keyPoints: BK virus is the most common polyomavirus affecting kidney transplant recipients.
Viruria is frequent; some patients develop viremia leading to PVAN and allograft loss if untreated.
Diagnosis: renal dysfunction + viremia (presumptive); histopathologic confirmation via viral cytopathic changes or immunohistochemistry for SV40/BKV-specific antigens.
Treatment involves reduction of immunosuppression; preemptive monitoring of BKV viral load can prevent PVAN.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · POLYOMAVIRUSES

items.title: JC Virus and Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy (PML) in Solid Organ Transplant Recipients
items.content: In solid organ transplant recipients, JC virus can cause progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML), a demyelinating disease of the CNS. Although less common than in other immunocompromised populations like those with advanced HIV, PML remains significant post-transplant. Diagnosis involves identifying compatible neurologic symptoms, molecular detection of JC virus in CSF, and radiologic signs of demyelination.
keyPoints: JC virus causes PML, a CNS disease, in solid organ transplant recipients.
PML is less common than in advanced HIV patients but significant post-transplant.
Diagnosis: compatible neurologic syndrome + JC virus detection in CSF + radiologic evidence of demyelination.

No effective antiviral therapy; reduction of immunosuppression has poor response rates. Directed T-cell therapies and immune checkpoint inhibitors like pembrolizumab are under development or have shown success.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · POLYOMAVIRUSES

items.title: Invasive Candidiasis in SOT Recipients
items.content: Invasive candidiasis is a common early complication post-SOT, with risk factors including prolonged hospitalization, broad-spectrum antimicrobials use, intravascular catheters, and complex abdominal surgeries. Targeted antifungal prophylaxis significantly reduces its incidence.
keyPoints: Most common invasive fungal infection in SOT recipients is invasive candidiasis.
Risk factors: prolonged hospitalization, broad-spectrum antimicrobials, intravascular catheters, renal replacement therapy, critical illness, complex abdominal surgeries.
Targeted antifungal prophylaxis decreases incidence of invasive candidiasis.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · FUNGAL INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.title: Management of Invasive Candidiasis
items.content: Echinocandins are the preferred initial treatment for invasive candidiasis in transplant recipients, except for CNS disease or urinary tract infection. Source control measures like catheter removal and mechanical drainage are crucial.
keyPoints: Initial treatment: echinocandins (except for CNS disease or UTI).
Source control: catheter removal, mechanical drainage of collections.
Lung transplant recipients risk mycotic aneurysms with high rupture risk.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · FUNGAL INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.title: Invasive Aspergillosis in Transplant Recipients
items.content: Invasive pulmonary aspergillosis is prevalent among lung transplant recipients, with risk factors including single-lung transplant and increased immunosuppression. Diagnosis involves targeted diagnostic BAL GMN and serum GMN, with CT imaging aiding early detection.
keyPoints: Most common invasive mold infection in lung transplant recipients: invasive pulmonary aspergillosis.
Risk factors: single-lung transplant, cystic fibrosis, airway ischemia, increased immunosuppression.
Diagnostic tools: targeted diagnostic BAL GMN, serum GMN; CT imaging for early diagnosis.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · FUNGAL INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.title: Cryptococcosis in SOT Recipients: Timing, Diagnosis, and Management
items.content: Solid organ transplant recipients are at risk for cryptococcal infections due to prolonged immunosuppression. These typically occur between 1 and 3 years post-transplant, but earlier presentations may indicate donor-derived infection or other risk factors like advanced liver disease. Serum and CSF cryptococcal antigen assays are crucial for early diagnosis, especially in CNS involvement. Lumbar puncture is recommended for patients with mental status changes to assess CSF involvement. Monitoring of cryptococcal antigen levels is not advised as they may persist despite resolution.
keyPoints: Cryptococcosis occurs 1-3 years post-transplant; earlier cases suggest donor-derived infection or advanced liver disease risk.
Serum and CSF cryptococcal antigen assays are essential for early diagnosis, particularly in CNS disease.
Lumbar puncture is necessary for patients with mental status changes to evaluate CSF involvement.
Cryptococcal antigen monitoring post-treatment is not recommended due to potential persistence despite resolution.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · FUNGAL INFECTIONS (part 2)

items.title: Pneumocystis jirovecii Pneumonia in SOT and Allo-HCT Recipients
items.content: PJP is a significant complication post-SOT and allo-HCT, with prophylaxis commonly used early after transplant. Breakthrough infections are rare with TMP-SMX but more common with second-line agents like inhaled pentamidine or atovaquone. Presentation includes acute/subacute shortness of breath with hypoxemia, often showing ground-glass opacities on imaging. Elevated $\beta$-D-glucan levels and PCR from BAL are diagnostic tools, though clinical correlation is needed due to potential colonization. Immunosuppression tapering is generally not advised during treatment due to risk of IRIS-like conditions.
keyPoints: PJP prophylaxis is routine post-SOT/allo-HCT; breakthroughs more likely with second-line agents.
Common presentation: acute/subacute shortness of breath, hypoxemia, ground-glass opacities on imaging.
Elevated $\beta$-D-glucan and PCR from BAL are key diagnostic tools, but clinical correlation is necessary.
Tapering immunosuppression during PJP treatment in transplant recipients is usually not advised due to IRIS risk.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · FUNGAL INFECTIONS (part 2)

items.title: High Incidence of TB in SOT Recipients
items.content: Solid organ transplant (SOT) recipients have a significantly higher incidence of active tuberculosis (TB), estimated to be 20–75 times greater than the general population. Most cases occur between 6 and 12 months post-transplant, with high mortality and graft loss.
keyPoints: Incidence of TB in SOT recipients: 20–75 times higher than general population
Most cases occur 6-12 months after transplant
High mortality and risk of graft loss
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.title: Treatment Duration for TB in Transplant Recipients
items.content: TB treatment in organ transplant recipients typically requires 6–9 months. However, disseminated disease with CNS involvement or other severe forms necessitate 12–18 months of therapy.
keyPoints: Standard TB treatment: 6–9 months
Extended treatment for disseminated disease/CNS involvement/extrapulmonary TB/culture positivity after 2 months: 12–18 months
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.title: Rifamycin Use and Drug Interactions in TB Treatment
items.content: Rifamycin-containing regimens are preferred for their potent sterilizing activity. Rifampin, a common rifamycin, induces cytochrome P450, increasing metabolism of immunosuppressive drugs and risking acute rejection.
keyPoints: Preferred use of rifamycin-containing regimens
Rifampin induces cytochrome P450, affecting drug levels of calcineurin inhibitors, mTOR inhibitors, corticosteroids
Risk of acute rejection with rifampin due to drug interactions
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.title: Treatment Timing and Strategy for LTBI in Transplant Recipients
items.content: All transplant recipients with latent tuberculosis infection (LTBI) should receive treatment. The timing of transplantation influences the choice of treatment duration, balancing efficacy against potential toxicities and drug interactions. If transplantation is more than 4-6 months away, shorter courses like rifampin for 4 months or isoniazid plus rifapentine for 12 weeks are suggested. For transplants expected within this timeframe, a longer course of isoniazid for 9 months is recommended. Treatment should ideally occur before transplantation unless it risks delaying the procedure.
keyPoints: All transplant recipients with LTBI should receive treatment.
Shorter courses (e.g., rifampin for 4 months or isoniazid plus rifapentine for 12 weeks) are suggested if the transplant is more than 4-6 months away.
Isoniazid for 9 months recommended if transplantation is expected within 4-6 months.
Treatment should ideally occur before transplantation but not delay it.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 2)

items.title: Management of NTM Infections in Transplant Recipients
items.content: Transplant recipients are at increased risk for nontuberculous mycobacteria (NTM) infections, particularly those with lung transplants. Pretransplant treatment is crucial and should begin at least 3 months prior to transplantation if colonization or infection is detected. Treatment regimens must be tailored based on species identification and susceptibility testing, considering drug-drug interactions and potential toxicities. Bilateral lung transplant is preferred for patients already colonized or infected with NTMs.
keyPoints: Transplant recipients are at increased risk of NTM infections, especially post-lung transplantation.
Pretransplant treatment should start at least 3 months prior to transplantation if colonization or infection is detected.
Treatment regimens must be tailored based on species identification and susceptibility testing.
Bilateral lung transplant preferred for patients already colonized or infected with NTMs.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 2)

items.title: Challenges in Treating Mycobacterial Infections in PID Patients Undergoing Transplant
items.content: Patients with primary immunodeficiencies (PIDs) susceptible to mycobacterial infections face high risks of disseminated NTM or TB. Successful management requires aggressive antimicrobial treatment before, during, and early after transplantation. Ideally, the infection should be partially controlled for at least 3 months prior to transplant. Drug-drug interactions and toxicities are significant challenges in peri- and posttransplant periods, with IRIS-like phenomena common in the initial months post-engraftment.
keyPoints: Patients with PIDs are highly susceptible to disseminated NTM or TB infections.
Aggressive antimicrobial treatment is required before, during, and early after transplantation.
Infection should be partially controlled for at least 3 months prior to transplant.
Drug-drug interactions and toxicities pose significant challenges in peri- and posttransplant periods.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 2)

items.title: Management of IRIS in Transplant Recipients
items.content: Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) presents a diagnostic challenge due to the absence of clear criteria. It is characterized by worsening symptoms or imaging findings at the site of prior infection, coinciding with tapering immunosuppression. Management involves treating the underlying infection and adjusting immunosuppressive therapy.
keyPoints: No definitive diagnostic criteria for IRIS.
Symptoms worsen or imaging changes occur as immunosuppression is tapered.
Management includes treatment of the underlying infection and adjustment of immunosuppression.
Tapering of immunosuppression should be gradual, considering other potential toxicities.

items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Nocardiosis in Immunocompromised Patients
items.content: Nocardia infections, primarily acquired through inhalation or skin inoculation, present variably from acute to chronic symptoms. Radiologic findings often include dense nodules with possible cavitation. CNS dissemination occurs in 25% of cases, frequently without symptoms. Brain MRI is recommended for all patients with Nocardia. Empirical treatment with linezolid is advised until species identification and susceptibilities are confirmed.
keyPoints: Nocardia is an aerobic gram-positive bacillus affecting immunocompromised individuals.
Infection acquired by inhalation or skin inoculation, with CNS dissemination in 25% of cases.
Brain MRI recommended to rule out brain abscess in all Nocardia patients.
Empirical treatment with linezolid; susceptibility testing may guide further therapy.

items.question: What is the most common site of nontuberculous mycobacteria (NTM) infection in transplant recipients?
items.explanation: The lungs are the most common site of NTM infection in transplant recipients.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 2)

items.question: What is a recommended treatment duration for pulmonary or disseminated NTM infections?
items.explanation: Pulmonary or disseminated NTMs require at least 1 year of treatment.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 2)

items.subtopic: MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 3) | NOCARDIOSIS | PREVENTION OF INFECTIONS IN TRANSPLANT RECIPIENTS
items.question: Which antibiotic is recommended for empirical treatment of Nocardia infections before species identification and susceptibility results are available?
items.explanation: All Nocardia species are susceptible to linezolid, making it suitable for empirical treatment.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 3) | NOCARDIOSIS | PREVENTION OF INFECTIONS IN TRANSPLANT RECIPIENTS

items.subtopic: MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 3) | NOCARDIOSIS | PREVENTION OF INFECTIONS IN TRANSPLANT RECIPIENTS
items.question: What is the recommended duration of antibiotic treatment for Nocardia infections?
items.explanation: Most experts recommend a long duration of treatment, usually 6–12 months.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 3) | NOCARDIOSIS | PREVENTION OF INFECTIONS IN TRANSPLANT RECIPIENTS

items.subtopic: MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 3) | NOCARDIOSIS | PREVENTION OF INFECTIONS IN TRANSPLANT RECIPIENTS
items.question: Which imaging modality is recommended for all patients with Nocardia to rule out brain abscess?
items.explanation: A Brain MRI is recommended in all patients with Nocardia to rule out brain abscess.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 3) | NOCARDIOSIS | PREVENTION OF INFECTIONS IN TRANSPLANT RECIPIENTS

items.subtopic: Infections in Transplant Recipients | GENERAL CONCEPTS
items.statement: Immunosuppressive agents are a common factor increasing the risk of infections across different types of transplants.
items.explanation: Immunosuppressive agents increase the risk of infection for all transplant recipients.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · Infections in Transplant Recipients | GENERAL CONCEPTS

items.subtopic: Infections in Transplant Recipients | GENERAL CONCEPTS
items.statement: Early infections after autologous HCT are primarily related to neutropenia.
items.explanation: Neutropenia-induced early infections occur after allogeneic HCT, not autologous HCT.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · Infections in Transplant Recipients | GENERAL CONCEPTS

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS AFTER HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION
items.statement: Auto-HCT involves stem cells from another person.
items.explanation: In auto-HCT, the stem cells are harvested from the recipient themselves.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · INFECTIONS AFTER HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS AFTER HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION
items.statement: GVHD is a common complication after allo-HCT.
items.explanation: Graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) affects between 35 and 50% of allo-HCT recipients.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · INFECTIONS AFTER HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION

items.statement: Persistent fever alone is an indication to modify the antibacterial regimen during neutropenic fever.
items.explanation: Persistent fever alone is not an indication to modify the antibacterial regimen; however, persistent fever while receiving broad-spectrum antibiotics may indicate a higher likelihood of invasive fungal disease.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 1)

items.statement: Antifungal prophylaxis is almost universal in the allo-HCT setting to prevent candidiasis.
items.explanation: Prophylaxis with fluconazole decreases the likelihood of invasive candidiasis and improves survival, making antifungal prophylaxis almost universal.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 1)

items.statement: Cytomegalovirus infection is common before engraftment.
items.explanation: CMV infection is uncommon before engraftment.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 2)

items.statement: Ex vivo T-cell depletion results in a product with more mature T cells than bone marrow.
items.explanation: Ex vivo T-cell depletion results in fewer mature T cells compared to bone marrow.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 2)

items.statement: Serologic markers of fungal infection are recommended for monitoring in patients on mold-active azoles.
items.explanation: The use of serologic markers of fungal infection is not recommended when on mold-active azoles due to reduced sensitivity.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 3)

items.statement: HHV-6 reactivation after HCT often causes severe encephalitis.
items.explanation: Most HHV-6 reactivations are asymptomatic and undetected; however, it can sometimes cause severe encephalitis.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 3)

items.statement: Ribavirin has a good response rate for established adenovirus disease.
items.explanation: The passage states that the response of established adenovirus disease to antivirals, including ribavirin, is poor.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 4)

items.statement: Influenza can be treated with amantadine and rimantadine in the United States.
items.explanation: Amantadine and rimantadine are not recommended due to widespread resistance.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 4)

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS FOR INFECTION AFTER SOLID ORGAN TRANSPLANT
items.statement: Induction immunosuppression is typically administered several weeks before transplantation.
items.explanation: Induction immunosuppression is usually administered immediately prior to transplantation.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · RISK FACTORS FOR INFECTION AFTER SOLID ORGAN TRANSPLANT

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS FOR INFECTION AFTER SOLID ORGAN TRANSPLANT
items.statement: Older patients are more likely to have encountered pathogens that can reactivate at the time of transplant.
items.explanation: The passage states older patients are more likely to have latent infections such as tuberculosis.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · RISK FACTORS FOR INFECTION AFTER SOLID ORGAN TRANSPLANT

items.subtopic: ☑ TIME COURSE OF INFECTIONS AFTER SOLID ORGAN TRANSPLANT (TABLE 148-2)
items.statement: Prophylaxis for opportunistic infections is typically stopped after the first month post-transplant.
items.explanation: Prophylaxis is usually started promptly after transplant and continued into the intermediate period.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · ☑ TIME COURSE OF INFECTIONS AFTER SOLID ORGAN TRANSPLANT (TABLE 148-2)

items.subtopic: ☑ TIME COURSE OF INFECTIONS AFTER SOLID ORGAN TRANSPLANT (TABLE 148-2)
items.statement: Patients with satisfactory allograft function are at higher risk of opportunistic infections in the late period.
items.explanation: The risk of opportunistic infections is lower in patients with satisfactory allograft function during the late period.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · ☑ TIME COURSE OF INFECTIONS AFTER SOLID ORGAN TRANSPLANT (TABLE 148-2)

items.statement: Fungal infections in VCAs can mimic rejection episodes.
items.explanation: Fungal infections, such as cryptococcus or mucocutaneous candidiasis, may mimic rejection.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · VASCULAR COMPOSITE ALLOGRAFTS

items.statement: Data regarding infection risk in VCAs are abundant and well-documented.
items.explanation: Data regarding infection risk in VCAs are still scarce and inferred from the known risk of SOT.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · VASCULAR COMPOSITE ALLOGRAFTS

items.statement: Donor bacteremia in deceased donors is generally considered a contraindication to organ procurement and transplantation.
items.explanation: Bacteremia is not usually a contraindication unless no effective antimicrobial therapy exists.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · DONOR-DERIVED INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Meningoencephalitis of unknown etiology in a donor is an absolute contraindication to transplantation.
items.explanation: Meningoencephalitis of unknown etiology in a donor remains one of the few remaining absolute contraindications.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · DONOR-DERIVED INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.statement: CMV viremia is usually symptomatic in SOT recipients.
items.explanation: CMV viremia is usually asymptomatic, although it can lead to CMV syndrome with symptoms.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · DONOR-DERIVED INFECTIONS (part 2) | CYTOMEGALOVIRUS (part 1)

items.statement: Active CMV replication can increase the risk of bacterial and fungal infections in transplant recipients.
items.explanation: Active CMV replication is associated with increased rates of bacterial and fungal infection.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · DONOR-DERIVED INFECTIONS (part 2) | CYTOMEGALOVIRUS (part 1)

items.statement: Cidofovir is considered superior to ganciclovir and foscarnet for treating significant CMV viremia.
items.explanation: Cidofovir is inferior and potentially more toxic compared to ganciclovir and foscarnet.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · CYTOMEGALOVIRUS (part 2)

items.statement: CMV pneumonia after allo-HCT has become uncommon with the use of prophylaxis or preemptive management.
items.explanation: Prophylaxis and preemptive management have made CMV pneumonia after allo-HCT uncommon.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · CYTOMEGALOVIRUS (part 2)

items.statement: The risk of PTLD after solid organ transplantation increases with the duration of immunosuppression.
items.explanation: Late PTLD incidence increases after 7–10 years and is related to the duration of immunosuppression.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · EPSTEIN-BARR VIRUS

items.statement: EBV D/R matching is not recommended in hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (allo-HCT).
items.explanation: EBV D/R matching is recommended whenever possible in allo-HCT.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · EPSTEIN-BARR VIRUS

items.statement: Effective antiviral therapy exists for JC virus infections in transplant recipients.
items.explanation: No effective antiviral therapy exists for JC virus infections; reduction of immunosuppression is commonly employed.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · POLYOMAVIRUSES

items.statement: BKV nephropathy can be clinically indistinguishable from acute rejection.
items.explanation: Acute rejection and BKV-associated nephropathy can coexist and may be clinically indistinguishable.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · POLYOMAVIRUSES

items.statement: Invasive candidiasis is most often a late complication after solid organ transplantation.
items.explanation: Invasive candidiasis is most often an early complication after SOT.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · FUNGAL INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.statement: CT imaging findings in SOT recipients with invasive aspergillosis are typically characterized by the halo sign.
items.explanation: The halo sign is almost never seen in SOT recipients; they more frequently present with nonspecific pulmonary findings.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · FUNGAL INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.statement: PJP prophylaxis is routinely used early after both SOT and allo-HCT.
items.explanation: Prophylaxis for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia is indeed used routinely early after both solid organ transplantation and allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · FUNGAL INFECTIONS (part 2)

items.statement: A positive PCR for PJP in a respiratory sample always indicates active infection.
items.explanation: A positive PCR may indicate colonization rather than active infection, requiring clinical interpretation.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · FUNGAL INFECTIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Rifampin is not associated with acute rejection in transplant recipients.
items.explanation: Rifampin use has been frequently associated with acute rejection due to its potent induction of cytochrome P450.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Intermittent dosing is recommended over daily therapy for TB treatment in transplant recipients.
items.explanation: Daily therapy is recommended over intermittent dosing due to higher relapse rates and concerns about drug-drug interactions with intermittent dosing.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Transplant recipients with LTBI should always delay transplantation to complete treatment.
items.explanation: The transplant should not be delayed to complete treatment for LTBI.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Rapidly growing NTMs, especially M. abscessus, are associated with a high risk of deep surgical site infection.
items.explanation: M. abscessus is indeed associated with a high risk of deep surgical site infection.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 2)

items.subtopic: MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 3) | NOCARDIOSIS | PREVENTION OF INFECTIONS IN TRANSPLANT RECIPIENTS
items.statement: Nocardia infections are typically acquired through ingestion.
items.explanation: Nocardia is more frequently acquired by inhalation or skin inoculation, not ingestion.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 3) | NOCARDIOSIS | PREVENTION OF INFECTIONS IN TRANSPLANT RECIPIENTS

items.subtopic: MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 3) | NOCARDIOSIS | PREVENTION OF INFECTIONS IN TRANSPLANT RECIPIENTS
items.statement: Dissemination of Nocardia to the CNS occurs in about 25% of cases.
items.explanation: The passage states that dissemination to the CNS occurs in 25% of cases.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 3) | NOCARDIOSIS | PREVENTION OF INFECTIONS IN TRANSPLANT RECIPIENTS

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS AFTER HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION
items.assertion: Allo-HCT can be considered a form of immunotherapy.
items.reason: It has the potential to cure chemotherapy-refractory malignancies through the graft-versus-leukemia effect.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why allo-HCT can be considered a form of immunotherapy.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · INFECTIONS AFTER HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS AFTER HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION
items.assertion: Auto-HCT is used to treat primary immunodeficiencies.
items.reason: It replaces defective stem cells with healthy ones from a donor.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because auto-HCT uses the recipient's own stem cells, not those from a donor.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · INFECTIONS AFTER HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION

items.assertion: The intensity of conditioning affects the duration of neutropenia in HCT.
items.reason: Different degrees and durations of neutropenia result from varying intensities of conditioning regimens.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why different conditioning intensities affect neutropenia duration.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 1)

items.assertion: Breakthrough infections can occur with fluconazole prophylaxis.
items.reason: Non-albicans Candida is resistant to fluconazole, leading to breakthrough infections.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why non-albicans Candida can cause breakthrough infections with fluconazole prophylaxis.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 1)

items.assertion: Patients with immunodeficiency or heavily treated for acute lymphoblastic leukemia carry considerable risk of early PJP.
items.reason: These patients are at higher risk due to their underlying conditions and prior treatments.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 2)

items.assertion: Acyclovir-resistant HSV should be considered if there is breakthrough infection despite prophylaxis.
items.reason: Drug resistance can only be determined definitively by phenotypic testing, which takes weeks.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why acyclovir-resistant HSV should be considered.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 2)

items.assertion: HHV-6 reactivation after HCT is usually asymptomatic.
items.reason: Routine monitoring of HHV-6 is not recommended because most reactivations do not cause symptoms.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why routine monitoring is not recommended.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 3)

items.assertion: Decreasing immunosuppression can help control viral infections after HCT.
items.reason: Viral infections require the host's specific immunity for long-term control, unlike bacterial infections.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why decreasing immunosuppression can help.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 3)

items.assertion: Nirmatrelvir-ritonavir is impractical for transplant recipients.
items.reason: Transplant recipients often take medications like tacrolimus or sirolimus, which interact with nirmatrelvir-ritonavir.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why nirmatrelvir-ritonavir is impractical for transplant recipients.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 4)

items.assertion: TMP-SMX may be equally effective as pyrimethamine and sulfadiazine in treating Toxoplasma gondii.
items.reason: TMP-SMX is the standard treatment for Toxoplasma gondii post-HCT.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false because pyrimethamine and sulfadiazine are the standard treatment.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · TIMELINE OF INFECTIONS AFTER HCT (part 4)

items.subtopic: ☑ TIME COURSE OF INFECTIONS AFTER SOLID ORGAN TRANSPLANT (TABLE 148-2)
items.assertion: Increased immunosuppression due to acute rejection increases the risk of opportunistic infections.
items.reason: Antimicrobial prophylaxes are often reset when an episode of acute rejection occurs, influencing infection risk.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · ☑ TIME COURSE OF INFECTIONS AFTER SOLID ORGAN TRANSPLANT (TABLE 148-2)

items.assertion: The risk of unexpected donor-derived infections is higher in cadaveric donations.
items.reason: Screening for transmissible pathogens is hampered by incomplete assessment of the donor's risk in cadaveric donation.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains why unexpected DDIs occur more frequently in cadaveric donations.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · DONOR-DERIVED INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Living donors with an identified active infection should have transplantation delayed until the infection resolves.
items.reason: Targeted antimicrobial prophylaxis can effectively prevent disease transmission from living donors with infections.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false because targeted antimicrobial prophylaxis is not typically used for active infections in living donors.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · DONOR-DERIVED INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Maribavir is FDA approved for resistant/refractory CMV.
items.reason: Its efficacy is only moderate, with less than 20% of patients maintaining control at 16 weeks.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why maribavir is FDA approved.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · CYTOMEGALOVIRUS (part 2)

items.assertion: The tolerable level of viremia after transplant differs based on type of transplant and timing.
items.reason: Randomized trials comparing prophylaxis and preemptive strategies are available for all types of transplants.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as randomized trials are not available for SOT involving organs other than kidney and liver.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · CYTOMEGALOVIRUS (part 2)

items.assertion: The risk of EBV-positive PTLD is highest in the first year after solid organ transplantation.
items.reason: EBV viral load monitoring can assist in early diagnosis and management.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why the risk is highest in the first year.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · EPSTEIN-BARR VIRUS

items.assertion: Donor lymphocyte infusion may be a successful strategy for managing EBV-positive PTLD after allo-HCT.
items.reason: The stem cell donor's T cells can control EBV if the donor is EBV positive.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains why donor lymphocyte infusion may be successful.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · EPSTEIN-BARR VIRUS

items.assertion: In lung transplant recipients, tracheobronchitis and bronchial anastomotic infections are characterized by ulceration and cartilage invasion.
items.reason: These conditions result from Aspergillus spp. pathology in the airway and allograft.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · FUNGAL INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Serum galactomannan monitoring performs well in SOT recipients for diagnosing invasive aspergillosis.
items.reason: It is a preferred diagnostic method when there is suspicion of invasive disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; serum galactomannan monitoring does not perform well in SOT recipients.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · FUNGAL INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Rifamycin-containing regimens are preferred for all types of TB in transplant recipients.
items.reason: These regimens have potent sterilizing activity, which is beneficial in treating tuberculosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why rifamycin-containing regimens are preferred.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Tuberculin skin test (TST) is always reliable in transplant recipients.
items.reason: The TST is less sensitive in immunocompromised hosts, which can lead to false-negative results.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the TST is not always reliable in transplant recipients due to reduced sensitivity.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Lung transplant candidates with cystic fibrosis are at increased risk of NTM disease after transplant.
items.reason: Pretransplant isolation of NTM is associated with increased risk of NTM disease after transplant.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: All patients with NTM infection pretransplant should start treatment after transplantation.
items.reason: Treatment before transplantation is recommended to reduce post-transplant infection risk.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; all patients with NTM infection pretransplant should start treatment before transplantation.
items.reference: Infections in Transplant Recipients · MYCOBACTERIAL INFECTIONS (part 2)
